Operation Alexandria

International Security Committee  ·  Crisis Directive  ·  Immediate Action Required
𓂀

The Alexandria Files

📍 Multi-Jurisdictional Crisis · INTERPOL Coordination Active · Restricted Distribution

Main Crisis Trigger

Submerged Structure Linked to the Library of Alexandria

An underwater archaeological expedition has discovered a submerged structure believed to be connected to the Library of Alexandria near the coast of Alexandria. Among the recovered artifacts are fragments of scrolls and tablets containing inscriptions similar to those historically associated with the original library.

Preliminary translation suggests several passages describe violent acts recorded in historical accounts. Authorities in multiple countries now report recent murders that closely resemble these descriptions. Each crime reflects an act described in the texts but differs by one element — such as the weapon used, the victim profile, the motive, or the method.

Analysts remain divided on the cause of these similarities. Preliminary cross-referencing has identified potential links to unresolved cases including the I-20 Killer and the English Butcher.

Theory A — Curse & Copycat The rediscovered texts may be inspiring copycat behavior tied to myths surrounding ancient Egyptian curses, attracting individuals who believe in or wish to enact the texts' prophesied power.
Theory B — Organized Replication Modern offenders may be deliberately replicating historical narratives — suggesting organized criminal activity, possibly international in scope, with ideological motivation.
  • Determine whether the murders represent organized international crime, individual copycat killings, or unrelated coincidences.
  • Decide how the recovered materials from the submerged site should be handled and protected.
  • Assess whether international coordination is required and define the scope of response.
Recovered Field Documentation — Site Alpha

Discovery Details

Archaeological Site Intelligence

🏛 Site Facts

  • Located within the submerged ruins of the ancient harbor of Alexandria
  • Archaeologists recovered fragments of scrolls, tablets, and coded inscriptions
  • Several inscriptions remain untranslated

🔴 Crime Pattern Observations

  • Murders with similar structures have appeared in multiple countries
  • Each crime matches a described act but differs by one variable
  • Some inscriptions appear to describe events that have not yet occurred

📡 Information Control

  • Only partial translations have been publicly released
  • Leaked fragments have begun circulating online
  • Some governments are requesting restricted access

🔎 Investigation Leads

  • The texts may reference historical crimes rather than mythology
  • A group could be using the texts as inspiration for copycat killings
  • Additional sites connected to the library may still exist

⚠ Potential Escalation Scenarios

  • New murders may appear that match additional untranslated inscriptions
  • A cult or extremist group could begin interpreting the texts as instructions
  • Full translation may reveal references to other historical locations or events
  • International media exposure of leaked fragments could trigger mass copycat interest
Background — The Library of Alexandria

Historical Context

The Library of Alexandria, founded in the 3rd century BCE under the Ptolemaic dynasty, was considered the greatest repository of knowledge in the ancient world. At its peak it is estimated to have housed between 400,000 and 700,000 scrolls spanning science, mathematics, philosophy, literature, and history.

The library's destruction remains one of history's most debated losses. Julius Caesar's campaign in 48 BCE, Aurelian's siege in 270 CE, and the Decree of Theophilus in 391 CE are among the events cited.

The ancient harbor district of Alexandria has been partially submerged by seismic activity over millennia. The scale and preservation of the newly discovered site is described as unprecedented by the archaeological team.

"They did not only bring together half a million books, but human beings also, on whom they spent great sums, in order to have about them men who were masters of every branch of learning."
— Strabo, Geography, 17.1.8
